worth_it_explanation: Account access is a security and accountability control. Access granted too broadly means more people can change bids, budgets, and billing than the work requires, and access that is never revoked leaves former staff or agencies able to reach a live account. The check is a quick read of the account's user list and access levels. The fix is to adjust or remove access, not to rebuild anything, and it closes a gap that is easy to overlook until something goes wrong.
intro: Google Ads grants each user an access level that sets what they can change. Admin access can manage users and billing, Standard access can edit campaigns but not users, and Read only or Email only access can view or receive reports without making changes. Access that is wider than the work needs, or access that outlives the relationship, is a risk to the account. These checks confirm that each user holds the lowest access their role requires and that no departed person or former agency still has a way in.

audit_checks.title: Users hold broader access than their role requires
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Admin access can change billing and add or remove other users, and Standard access can change every bid and budget. When people who only need to view reports hold those levels, a mistaken or unauthorized change can reach spend and billing, and the account has more ways to be altered than the work justifies.

educate.base:
Every user on a Google Ads account holds an access level that decides what they can do. Admin access can edit the account and also manage users and billing. Standard access can edit campaigns, bids, and budgets but cannot manage users. Read only access can view the account and pull reports, and Email only access receives reports without signing in.

The principle of least access is to grant each person the lowest level their work requires. When a stakeholder who only reviews performance holds Admin or Standard access, the account carries risk it does not need: that person can change spend, and an Admin can add further users or alter billing. The risk is not about trust alone. A well-meaning person can still make a change in the wrong account or click the wrong control.

The user list lives in the account's access and security settings. A few signs point to access that is too broad:
  • People who only read reports hold Standard or Admin access rather than Read only
  • More than one or two people hold Admin access without a clear need to manage users or billing
  • Access levels do not map to what each person actually does in the account
Rightsizing each user to the lowest level their role needs reduces the surface for an accidental or unauthorized change. You may want to confirm with the client what each person's job in the account is before lowering anyone's access, so a reduction does not block real work.

site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, sales or operations stakeholders often want visibility into cost per lead without needing to edit campaigns. You may want to move those viewers to Read only access so they keep the reporting they rely on without the ability to change spend.

learn_more.source_title: About access levels in your Google Ads account
Manage access to your Google Ads account

investigate.intro: Review the user list and confirm each person holds the lowest access level their role requires.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the account's access and security settings and list every user with their access level.
For each user, note what they actually do in the account, for example edit campaigns, manage billing, or only view reports.
Flag any user whose access level is higher than their work requires, for example a report viewer with Standard or Admin access.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per user, their access level and the work their role requires. A user whose access matches the lowest level their work needs passes. A user holding access wider than their role requires is a fail recorded with the user and the level that would fit.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each user's access level and the work their role requires to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} users on {{site_name}}'s Google Ads account who hold broader access than their role requires, such as report viewers with Standard or Admin access. The wider access lets more people change bids, budgets, or billing than the work justifies, which raises the chance of an accidental or unauthorized change reaching spend. {{voice_recommend}} lowering each user to the least access their role needs, confirming with the client what each person does before reducing anyone, so the change protects the account without blocking real work.

audit_checks.title: Departed staff or former agencies still have account access
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Access that outlives the relationship leaves a live account reachable by people who no longer work on it. A former employee or agency can still view sensitive performance data or change the account, and the longer the access sits unused, the less likely anyone is watching it.

educate.base:
Access to a Google Ads account persists until someone removes it. When an employee leaves, an agency engagement ends, or a freelancer finishes a project, their access stays in place unless it is revoked. The account then has users who no longer have a reason to reach it.

Stale access is a security gap. A former agency can still see budgets and performance, and a departed employee with Standard or Admin access can still change the account or, in the Admin case, add other users. Because the access is unused day to day, no one is watching it, so a problem can sit unnoticed. The risk grows with the sensitivity of what the account holds, including customer-match data and spend.

The user list shows everyone with access and when each was added. A few signs point to access that should be revoked:
  • Email domains in the user list belong to a former agency or a person who has left
  • Users appear who no one on the current team recognizes
  • Access was granted for a finite project that has since ended
Removing access for anyone outside the current working relationship closes the gap. You may want to confirm each unfamiliar user with the client before removing them, so an active partner is not cut off by mistake.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, a former agency with lingering access can still see revenue and margin data through conversion-value reporting. You may want to revoke any past agency's access promptly so commercial performance is not visible to a party no longer under contract.

learn_more.source_title: Secure your Google Ads account: best practices
Manage access to your Google Ads account

investigate.intro: Review the user list for anyone outside the current working relationship and confirm whether their access should be removed.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the access and security settings and list every user and their email domain.
Compare the list against the current team and any active agency or contractor.
Flag any user who belongs to a former agency, has left the business, or is not recognized by the current team.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per user, whether they are part of the current working relationship. A user who is current passes. A user from a former agency, a departed employee, or an unrecognized account is a fail recorded with the user, pending the client's confirmation before removal.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each user against the current team and active partners to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} users on {{site_name}}'s Google Ads account who are outside the current working relationship, such as a former agency or a departed employee whose access was never revoked. That standing access lets people who no longer work on the account view sensitive performance data or change it, and because it goes unused day to day, no one is watching it. {{voice_recommend}} confirming each unfamiliar user with the client and then removing access for anyone outside the current relationship, so only the people doing the work can reach the account.
